title: Damping: when the wobble dies
scenario: Add friction to the mass on a spring. As the damping grows the motion changes character — oscillating, then just barely returning, then crawling back. Where is the boundary, and what is the fastest a system can return to rest?

assumptions.claim: Linear (viscous) damping: the resistive force is -b·x' (proportional to velocity).
Ideal Hooke's-law spring; the mass is a point.

steps.label: What the algebra-based course can't give you
steps.prose: Algebra can name the three cases and the critical damping b_c = 2√(km), but it cannot produce the motion — the acceleration depends on both position and velocity, so only the differential equation has the answer.

steps.label: Newton's law with a spring and a drag force
steps.prose: Restoring force −kx plus a velocity-dependent damping −bx'. Here γ = b/2m and ω₀² = k/m.

steps.label: The form of the solution depends on the damping
steps.latex: \zeta<1:\ e^{-\gamma t}\cos\omega_d t \quad \zeta=1:\ (A+Bt)e^{-\gamma t} \quad \zeta>1:\ e^{-\gamma t}\cosh st
steps.prose: Underdamped: oscillation inside a decaying envelope, ω_d = √(ω₀²−γ²). Critically damped: the fastest return without overshoot. Overdamped: a slow, non-oscillating crawl back.

steps.label: Energy leaks out
steps.prose: Unlike the undamped oscillator (where energy is conserved), the damping removes energy at the rate b v² — and SymPy proves it.

steps.label: The transition is the lesson
steps.prose: Slide the damping through critical and watch oscillation give way to a dead-beat return. Each stop on the slider is an exact, separately verified solution.

proof.heading: All three damping regimes provably solve the equation of motion, and energy dissipates.
proof.detail: back-substitute each form into m x'' + b x' + k x = 0; check ICs; check dE/dt = −b v²

checks.claim: Underdamped form e^{−γt}(cos, sin) solves x'' + 2γx' + ω₀²x = 0.
Critical form (x₀+…t)e^{−γt} solves x'' + 2γx' + γ²x = 0 (ω₀ = γ).
Overdamped form e^{−γt}(cosh, sinh) solves x'' + 2γx' + (γ²−s²)x = 0.
It matches the initial position x(0) = x₀.
It matches the initial velocity x'(0) = v₀.
Energy dissipates at exactly dE/dt = −b v² (here per unit mass, b/m = 2γ).

misconception.claim: More damping always makes a system return to rest faster.
misconception.refuted_by: Past critical damping, adding more damping makes the return SLOWER — the overdamped system crawls back to equilibrium. The quickest return without overshoot is exactly at critical damping (ζ = 1); that is why door closers and instrument needles are tuned to it.
